claim_statement: In Romans 3:24, the verb δικαιόω ("dikaioumenoi" / "being justified") is used primarily in a forensic, declarative sense (God's legal declaration of righteousness), rather than in a primarily transformative or intrinsic-renewal sense.
resolution_summary: Both Reformed and Catholic traditions agree that δικαιόω in Romans 3:24 is central to Paul's soteriology and involves God's gracious action toward sinners. However, they diverge fundamentally on whether the term primarily denotes a forensic/legal declaration (Reformed) or includes an intrinsic transformation of the believer (Catholic). This divergence stems from different hermeneutical priors regarding the relationship between forensic language and transformative reality, and how to harmonize Paul with James.

root.summary: Both traditions agree that Romans 3:24 describes God's gracious action toward sinners using the passive participle δικαιούμενοι, and that this action is central to Christian soteriology.

depends_on.notes: The Greek text δικαιούμενοι δωρεὰν τῇ αὐτοῦ χάριτι is the shared textual basis.

divergence_point.label: Semantic range of δικαιόω
divergence_point.description: Whether δικαιόω primarily denotes forensic declaration or includes intrinsic transformation as a necessary component.

supporting_evidence.text_quoted: dikaioumenoi dwrean th autou cariti dia thv apolutrwsewv thv en cristw ihsou

children.summary: Reformed tradition interprets δικαιόω as primarily forensic/legal declaration, consistent with Calvin's Institutes 3.11.2 and Westminster Confession 11.1, emphasizing imputation rather than infusion.

depends_on.notes: Reformed interpretation prioritizes Paul's forensic language and treats James as addressing different aspects of faith.

supporting_evidence.text_quoted: A man is said to be justified in the sight of God when in the judgment of God he is deemed righteous, and is accepted on account of his righteousness. And this justification consists in the forgiveness of sins and the imputation of the righteousness of Christ.
Those whom God effectually calleth, He also freely justifieth: not by infusing righteousness into them, but by imputing the righteousness of Christ unto them.
For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God
There is no difference, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God

counter_evidence.text_quoted: Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only
Justification itself, which is not remission of sins merely, but also the sanctification and renewal of the inward man

children.notes: Reformed position treats forensic declaration as logically prior to and distinct from sanctification, though not separable in practice.

children.summary: Catholic tradition interprets δικαιόω as including both forensic declaration and real transformation, following Trent Session 6 Chapter 7, which explicitly rejects justification as 'merely remission of sins' and affirms 'sanctification and renewal of the inward man'.

depends_on.notes: Catholic interpretation requires harmonizing Paul and James as complementary, not contradictory.

supporting_evidence.text_quoted: Justification itself, which is not remission of sins merely, but also the sanctification and renewal of the inward man, through the voluntary reception of the grace, and of the gifts, whereby man of unjust becomes just
But God be thanked, that ye were the servants of sin... and being made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness
Ye see then how that by works a man is justified, and not by faith only

counter_evidence.text_quoted: There is no difference, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God
justification consists in the forgiveness of sins and the imputation of the righteousness of Christ

children.notes: Catholic position treats justification as a process that includes both declaration and transformation as integral aspects of God's gracious action.

root.notes: The fundamental divergence stems from different hermeneutical frameworks for handling apparent tensions between Pauline and Jacobite texts.

profile_outcomes.rationale_summary: Reformed tradition affirms the forensic interpretation as primary, grounded in Calvin's Institutes and Westminster Confession, with Paul's language taking precedence in cases of apparent tension with James.
Catholic tradition rejects the purely forensic interpretation, following Trent's explicit teaching that justification includes both forgiveness and renewal, requiring harmonization of Paul and James.

metrics.notes: High stability due to clear historical positions; deep consensus only at root level; strong contestation persists on the semantic range of δικαιόω; minimal drift expected as positions are well-established.

version_history.note: Initial resolution tree synthesizing Round 1 debate moves for romans-3-24-dikaioo-forensic claim.
